cards.back: Data is any information that can be moved, processed, or stored by a computer. Programs manipulate data by reading, changing, and writing it.

cards.text: A single piece of data stored in memory is called a {{c1::value}}.

cards.back: RAM is the memory available for programs to use. When a program is run, the operating system loads it into RAM. Data that needs to be stored beyond program termination must be written to disk.

cards.front: What is an object in C++?
cards.back: An object is a region of storage (usually memory) that can store a value and has other associated properties (like a type).

cards.text: A {{c1::variable}} is a named object. The name of the object is called an {{c2::identifier}}.
The statement to create a variable is called a {{c1::definition}} (or {{c2::definition statement}}).

cards.question: What does this statement do?
cards.answer: Defines a variable named x of type int (integer)
cards.explanation: This is a definition statement that creates a variable. 'int' is the type, and 'x' is the identifier (name) of the variable.

cards.front: What is a data type (or type) in C++?
cards.back: A data type tells the compiler what type of value (integer, letter, decimal number, etc.) a variable will store. It also determines how much memory to allocate for the variable.

cards.text: The {{c1::int}} type is used to store integer (whole number) values, such as {{c2::4}}, {{c3::27}}, or {{c4::-1234}}.

cards.question: How many variables are defined in this statement?
cards.explanation: You can define multiple variables of the same type in a single statement by separating the names with commas. However, this is not recommended as it can lead to mistakes.

cards.concept: Best practice for defining multiple variables
cards.explanation:
Although C++ allows you to define multiple variables of the same type in a single statement (e.g., int a, b;), it's better to define each variable in a separate statement on its own line:
  • Easier to read
  • Easier to comment individual variables
  • Prevents mistakes with mixed definitions
examples:
// Error-prone:
int a, double b; // Won't compile!

cards.text: At runtime, the variable will be {{c1::instantiated}}, which means the object will be created and assigned a memory address.
